questions.prompt: Complete: "Look at those two players — they ___ score any minute now!" (evidence from what you see)
questions.explanation: A prediction based on visible evidence uses be going to: they are going to score. Will to is never correct — will never takes to. Is going to uses the singular be, wrong for they. Going to alone drops the required be (are).

questions.prompt: Complete: "A: These bags are very heavy! B: Don't worry, I ___ carry them for you." (deciding now)
questions.explanation: An offer or decision made at the moment of speaking uses will + base verb: I will carry them. Am going to suggests a pre-made plan, not a spontaneous offer. Will to adds an incorrect to after will. Going to carry is missing the required be (am).

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ you going to call her, or should I?"
questions.explanation: A going-to question inverts the verb be with the subject. With you, the form of be is Are: Are you going to call? Is is for he/she/it, not you. Will would start a different structure (Will you call?). Do is the helper for present simple — it does not go with going to.

options.text: They are going to open a new café next month.
I think she will pass the exam.
He will going to travel to London next week.
We won't be there on time.

questions.explanation: The error is in the third sentence: will and going to cannot be stacked together. Say either he is going to travel or he will travel. The first sentence correctly uses going to for a plan. The second uses will for an opinion prediction. The fourth correctly uses the negative won't.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I've already chosen my university course. I ___ study computer science."
questions.explanation: A plan already decided before the moment of speaking uses be going to: I am going to study. The phrase I've already chosen confirms a pre-made decision. Will would suggest a decision made right now, not an earlier one. Going to alone is missing the be (am). Will to adds an incorrect to.

options.text: Will you to help me move this furniture?
Are you going help me move this furniture?
Will you help me move this furniture?
Are you will to help me move this furniture?

questions.explanation: A will question is Will + subject + base verb: Will you help? The first option adds a wrong to after will. The second is a going-to question missing to (should be going to help). The fourth mixes are and will, which cannot go together.
